role_hints.oews_basis: 47-2152 Plumbers, Pipefitters, and Steamfitters 26.4% of wage bill (5-digit 23822 mix — clean, code-specific)
role_hints.note: Licensed physical trade work — installation, repair, code-compliant pipefitting. AI does not turn wrenches; the exposure inside this role is limited to job documentation, permit paperwork and diagnostic assistance, a small fraction.

role_hints.oews_basis: 49-9021 Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers 20.4% + 47-2211 Sheet Metal Workers 4.1%
role_hints.note: Physical install/service work, EPA 608 certification for refrigerants. AI-assisted diagnostics and manuals-on-demand raise wrench-time productivity modestly; substitution is near zero.
Physical labor pipeline for the trades; unaffected by current AI except training delivery.

role_hints.oews_basis: 47-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades 5.8% + 49-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics 2.7%
role_hints.note: Crew coordination and quality control are on-site; scheduling, job costing and progress reporting automate substantially.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 6.0% + estimators/project managers in the unlisted tail, est. ~3-5%
role_hints.note: Takeoffs, bid assembly and commercial estimating are heavily AI-tractable; negotiation with GCs and key-account management persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 2.3% + comfort-advisor roles in the tail, est. ~2-3%
role_hints.note: Lead qualification, quote generation and financing paperwork automate; in-home close of $10-20K replacement jobs stays human under current practice.

role_hints.role: Office: dispatch, CSR and back office
role_hints.oews_basis: 43-9061 Office Clerks 2.9% + dispatchers, CSRs, bookkeepers and permit clerks in the unlisted tail, est. ~10-14%
role_hints.note: The AI target zone: call answering/booking (AI voice agents live in the industry now), dispatch optimization, invoicing, permit filing, maintenance-agreement renewals and review management automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: ServiceTitan 'State of Home Services' / trades industry reports
source_hints.why: The dominant field-service software vendor publishes adoption data on automation and AI features across residential trades — closest thing to an adoption benchmark.

source_hints.source: ACCA (Air Conditioning Contractors of America) and PHCC (Plumbing-Heating-Cooling Contractors Association) member surveys
source_hints.why: Trade-association primary data on technology use, workforce shortage and business practices for this exact code.

source_hints.source: PHCC / ACCA workforce and ownership commentary; Service Nation / Nexstar peer-group materials
source_hints.why: Aging-owner succession is the industry's own narrative (median contractor owner near 60 is widely claimed) — verify with association data rather than repeating the claim.

source_hints.source: OPTIS-style trades M&A trackers: Capstone Partners / Houlihan Lokey home-services M&A reports
source_hints.why: Investment-bank coverage of the residential-services roll-up wave with EBITDA multiple ranges by size tier — the sourced multiple evidence for M.

source_hints.source: Named platform activity: Apex Service Partners, Wrench Group, Redwood Services, Sila Services, Leap Partners, Heartland Home Services, plus strategics (ARS, Service Experts)
source_hints.why: Documented active consolidators for B; press releases and PE announcements verify current pace.
Main-street multiples for sub-$1M-EBITDA shops — the buy-side anchor, versus bank-reported platform multiples for exits.

source_hints.source: EPA regulations (Section 608, refrigerant transition/A2L) and state licensing boards
source_hints.why: The regulatory gates (licensing, refrigerant handling, permits) that block AI bypass and force paid demand through licensed contractors.

capture_questions: Residential service runs on flat-rate menu pricing (price-book per task, not hourly cost-plus) and replacement jobs are quoted competitively per install — when AI removes CSR/dispatch/admin cost, does the operator keep it as margin under flat-rate pricing, or does local competition (and private-equity-scaled competitors with the same AI) re-anchor the price book downward?

Commercial/new-construction work is bid cost-plus-ish against GCs — does that segment's competitive bidding pass AI estimating savings to the GC, and what is this operator's residential-service vs. commercial-construction revenue mix?

Who owns the customer — the brand (maintenance agreements, recurring service contracts held by the firm) or nobody (one-off transactional service where lead-gen platforms like Google LSA/Angi own demand and charge for it)? What share of revenue is under membership/maintenance agreements?

What stops homeowners from using AI to self-diagnose and buy parts, bypassing the contractor — and do licensing, permit requirements, refrigerant rules (EPA 608) and equipment-warranty terms make DIY bypass structurally marginal?

Do lead-gen platforms and OEM dealer programs (Carrier/Trane dealer networks) capture the AI-era margin upstream — raising per-lead prices as contractor costs fall — leaving pi_dist thinner than the flat-rate story suggests?
terminal_value_question: The service is a licensed human with tools physically installing or repairing equipment in a building — mature AI cannot do the act, and permits/licensing/refrigerant law (state contractor licensing, EPA 608) legally reserve it — so AI is a cost-cutter here, not a product-replacer; the honest terminal risks are demand-shape, not substitution: heat-pump electrification churn (a tailwind), smart-equipment self-diagnostics reducing service calls, OEM remote monitoring capturing the service relationship, and AI-armed national platforms competing away local margin. Is there any mechanism by which paid demand for licensed on-site work shrinks — or does the wage bill's ~60%+ trade-labor share simply cap how much AI can ever save this operator?
special_notes: The automatable slice is structurally small: ~57% of the wage bill is trade labor and helpers (47-2152, 49-9021, 47-2211, helpers) with near-zero AI substitution, plus ~9% field supervision — the AI story lives in the ~15-20% office/sales/estimating layer. Cap V accordingly; this is the golden-set winner anchor for physical trades and overstating within-role fractions here is the instrument's failure mode.

EBITDA normalization: owner comp inside SDE at the small end; seasonality (cooling/heating seasons) distorts TTM snapshots; new-construction exposure is cyclical with housing starts — separate residential service/replacement (the roll-up target economics) from new-construction sub work (lower margin, cyclical) before applying multiples.

Multiples are size-tiered and well documented: main-street shops (<$1M EBITDA) trade at low-to-mid single-digit SDE/EBITDA multiples while PE platforms have paid ~7-12x+ for scaled residential-services businesses — quote both tiers with sources (bank reports, BizBuySell) and do not let peak-2021/22 platform prints stand un-verified for the current market.

Labor-share caveat: the record's labor_share (0.3508) is computed at ancestor level 23822 and marked LOW quality, and the n_band chain applies Damodaran's 7.96% Engineering/Construction margin — residential service/replacement shops run materially higher EBITDA margins (10-20%), so n_band (1,152, from the 100-499 class only) likely understates band-eligible firms; the 9,415-firm 20-99 class contains many true $1-3M-EBITDA targets. Flag when scoring N.

Boundary confusion: electrical contractors are 238210, roofing 238160, appliance repair 811412, mechanical GCs and fire-sprinkler contractors also sit inside 238220's commercial side — association data (ACCA=HVAC, PHCC=plumbing) each cover only part of the code, and 'HVAC industry' market sizes usually exclude plumbing. The code blends residential service, commercial mechanical and new-construction subcontracting: score the DOMINANT sub-segment (residential + light-commercial service/replacement) and note heterogeneity.
Consolidation wave (Apex, Wrench, Redwood, Sila, et al.) is usable for B and M only — never as evidence for V or A (rule 2). Verify current deal pace; the 2021-2023 tempo has cooled with rates.

Chronic technician shortage cuts both ways: it supports pricing power (winner logic) but means AI cannot relieve the binding constraint (licensed labor) — AI savings land on the small office slice while the scarce input keeps inflating; model net margin effect honestly.
